items.title: Photo app auto-tagging
items.scenario: A phone gallery trains a convolutional neural network on millions of labeled photos. Early layers learn edge detectors; deeper layers combine them into object detectors. At inference, a new photo flows through the stack and the model outputs tags like beach, dog, sunset without anyone hand-writing color histogram rules.
items.explanation: Representation learning replaces manual image features. The hierarchy emerges from data, which is the hallmark of deep learning versus classical feature engineering.

items.title: Voice assistant wake-word detection
items.scenario: A smart speaker uses a deep acoustic model on raw audio spectrograms. The network learns phoneme-like units and phrase patterns from thousands of hours of speech, then classifies whether the user said the wake word.
items.explanation: Audio is high-dimensional and temporal; deep nets learn time-frequency representations that are hard to specify by hand.

items.title: Fraud detection with mixed signals
items.scenario: A bank combines tabular transaction fields with short merchant-description text. A deep model embeds text tokens and merges them with numeric features in hidden layers to score fraud risk.
items.explanation: DL is not only for images and language — embedding layers learn representations for categorical and text fields inside an end-to-end scorer.

items.title: When classical ML still wins
items.scenario: A factory has 800 labeled rows of sensor readings and needs a interpretable defect predictor tomorrow. A gradient-boosted tree on hand-picked statistics (mean vibration, peak temperature) trains in minutes and ships with feature importance reports.
items.explanation: Small tabular datasets with strong domain features often favor classical ML. Deep learning shines when scale and representation learning justify compute and data needs.
